name: Write a 2000-word technical blog post from research notes
description: An engineering team spent 3 weeks migrating from MongoDB to Postgres. They have rough notes: the reasons (query complexity, cost), the pain points (dual-write period, data reconciliation), the wins (50% cost reduction, simpler joins), and 3 gotchas they wish they'd known. They want a polished public-facing blog post.
task:
Write a 2000-word technical blog post titled 'How we migrated from MongoDB to Postgres without downtime'. Source material (raw notes from the team):

- Reasons: join queries became painful, MongoDB was 3x more expensive at our scale, aggregation pipelines were hard to maintain
- Strategy: dual-write period for 2 weeks, then backfill with a Python script, then cut over
- Pain points: handling unique constraints that MongoDB allowed as dupes, restructuring embedded docs into separate tables, reconciling timestamps across timezones
- Wins: 50% cost drop, 80% less code in aggregation layer, better tooling
- Gotchas: (1) sequence ID wraparound we didn't plan for, (2) pg_bouncer session mode vs transaction mode, (3) VACUUM disruption during backfill

Produce a ready-to-publish post. Real voice (first-person team perspective), concrete code examples for at least one gotcha, clear structure, actionable lessons for readers, no filler. Target audience: senior backend engineers.
expected_outcomes: Clear structure: motivation, approach, challenges, results, lessons
Concrete code example for at least one gotcha
Uses the real stats (50% cost, 80% code reduction)
All 3 gotchas surfaced with actionable lessons
